title: The new political logic of global tech and trade
gist: Globalisation is no longer treated as politically neutral: governments now view integrated technology networks (semis, EVs, AI, batteries) as national-security assets. The deeper divide is not globalisation vs deglobalisation but a contest over who occupies the most valuable nodes — and China is using self-reliance not to retreat but to expand influence within next-gen industrial networks.

insights.claim: The Pentagon adding Alibaba (e-commerce), BYD (EVs) and Baidu (search/AI) to a list of firms allegedly linked to China's military signals that the boundary between commercial activity and strategic competition is disappearing.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Any Chinese consumer-tech name can be reclassified as a security risk overnight; pricing in dual-use designation risk is now table stakes for China tech exposure.
insights.quote: The boundary between commercial activity and strategic competition appears to be disappearing.
insights.locator: When the Pentagon recently added companies Alibaba, BYD and Baidu

insights.claim: The post-Cold War assumption that globalisation is a largely economic phenomenon — markets generating efficiency, interdependence reducing conflict — is under strain because technology, supply chains, finance and industrial capabilities are now viewed as sources of leverage, dependence and vulnerability rather than politically neutral.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: The interdependence-reduces-conflict thesis underpinning decades of cross-border capital allocation is being repudiated by states; expect persistent securitisation premia on trade-exposed assets.
insights.locator: For much of the post-Cold War era, globalisation was presented

insights.claim: Governments now target integrated technology networks rather than individual firms because the strategic value of a semiconductor, EV or AI capability lies in the whole network — design software, fabrication equipment, talent, cloud, batteries, minerals, compute, energy — not in any single component.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Edge sits in choke-point components within the network (EDA, litho, minerals processing); map the full stack, not the headline name, when assessing strategic value or vulnerability.
insights.quote: The strategic value lies not in any single component but in the network as a whole.

insights.claim: Beijing treats strategic competition as an incentive to occupy more valuable positions within global technology networks rather than as a reason to restrict flows, with self-reliance a means to expand influence abroad, not the destination.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: China decoupling is a misread; the trade is Chinese firms gaining standard-setting and node-control positions in EVs, batteries and AI infrastructure globally.
insights.quote: Beijing treats it as an incentive to occupy more valuable positions within them.
insights.locator: Rather than viewing strategic competition primarily

insights.claim: Huawei's unveiling of the Tau Scaling Law matters less as import-substitution than as an attempt to establish alternative technological pathways that attract broad international participation across research, industry and innovation networks.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Watch whether non-US AI labs/hardware ecosystems adopt Huawei pathways — adoption would signal a parallel AI stack diluting US frontier-lab leverage.
insights.locator: Huawei's recent unveiling of the Tau Scaling Law

insights.claim: Ford licensing battery technology from China's CATL inverts a century of technology flow, with an iconic American manufacturer now dependent on Chinese battery technology to stay competitive in EVs, and CATL's significance lying in its position across critical minerals, processing, manufacturing and battery innovation.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: US automakers are structurally long Chinese battery IP; CATL's network position is a durable choke-point worth tracking for both Ford margins and policy-risk headlines.
insights.quote: one of America's most iconic manufacturers relies on Chinese battery technology to remain competitive in the electric vehicle market
insights.locator: Ford's decision to license battery technology

insights.claim: Chinese firms are exporting whole factories worldwide, which amounts to exporting suppliers, standards, technologies and industrial know-how, not just production capacity.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Standards capture travels with exported factories; downstream supplier ecosystems in host countries become locked into Chinese norms, a slow-burn moat.
insights.quote: They are also exporting suppliers, standards, technologies and industrial know-how.
insights.locator: A similar dynamic is visible in manufacturing

insights.claim: China runs a dual strategy of protecting strategic capabilities at home (including scrutiny of foreign involvement in Chinese tech firms) while encouraging outward expansion of firms and industrial networks, whereas Washington and the West focus mainly on managing risk through screening, restrictions and safeguards.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Defensive Western posture vs offensive Chinese expansion means the West may win the controls game yet lose node-occupancy; favour exposure to Chinese standard-setters in green tech.
insights.quote: selective control over strategic assets at home combined with the expansion of influence abroad
insights.locator: Beijing's outward orientation does not tell the whole story

insights.claim: The emerging divide in globalisation is over who occupies its most valuable nodes and whose industrial systems set the terms of participation, not between globalisation and deglobalisation.
insights.speaker: G. Venkat Raman
insights.so_what: Frame portfolio geopolitics around node-control and standards leadership rather than open-vs-closed; the winners own the chokepoints.
insights.quote: who gets to occupy its most valuable nodes and whose industrial systems ultimately set the terms of participation
insights.locator: The emerging divide is therefore not between
